findings.text: Major | content — slide 2 title — title "Solution Overview" labels a topic instead of stating a claim — Pyramid Principle (Minto 1987) — Rewrite title as the slide's core claim, e.g., "Our hybrid solution cuts onboarding from 6 weeks to 4 days"
findings.rationale: Action titles state the slide's claim; topic labels like "Overview" force the audience to derive the message themselves.
findings.standard: Pyramid Principle (Minto 1987) — title states the answer
findings.fix: Rewrite as a claim that names the answer, e.g., "Our hybrid solution cuts onboarding from 6 weeks to 4 days"

findings.text: Major | content — slide 2 body — claim "innovative and disruptive" carries no supporting evidence (no data, source, or example) — Heath, Made to Stick 2007 — Replace adjectives with a quantified claim and a source citation
findings.rationale: Hyperbolic adjectives ("innovative", "disruptive") with no data, customer story, or third-party citation are unsupported claims; the audience has no anchor to evaluate the assertion.
findings.fix: Replace with a quantified outcome and source, e.g., "Reduced onboarding 86% (Acme pilot, Q2 2026)"

findings.text: Major | content — slide 4 title — title "Strategic Direction" labels a topic instead of stating a claim — Pyramid Principle (Minto 1987) — Rewrite as the slide's claim
findings.rationale: Action titles state the slide's claim; "Strategic Direction" is a topic label that requires the audience to read the body to find the answer.
findings.standard: Pyramid Principle (Minto 1987) — title states the answer
findings.fix: Rewrite title as a claim that summarizes the recommended direction

findings.text: Major | content — slide 4 bullet 1 — bullet is 30 words (>25 word threshold) — 6×6 rule heuristic (Reynolds, Presentation Zen 2008) — Split into shorter claims or move detail to speaker notes
findings.rationale: Bullets >25 words become paragraphs; audience reads instead of listening, breaking presenter-audience connection.
findings.fix: Split into two bullets, OR move detail to speaker notes, OR rewrite as a single short claim

findings.text: Major | content — slide 4 body — bullet contains 7 distinct unfiltered acronyms (SaaS, B2B, SMB, GTM, PLG, ARR, ACV, ICP, MQLs, SQLs, BCG) — audience-fit (Knaflic, Storytelling with Data 2015) — Spell out acronyms on first use; cap at ≤5 distinct per slide
findings.rationale: Acronym density above 5/slide forces the audience into glossary lookups and erodes standalone-readability.
findings.fix: Spell out acronyms on first use; move definitions to speaker notes; keep ≤5 distinct acronyms per slide
